symbols: Mary ascending on clouds — bodily assumption into heavenly glory
angels supporting her — the heavenly escort receiving the Mother of God
apostles gazing upward — the earthly Church witnessing the heavenly mystery
empty space below — Mary’s empty tomb, paralleling Christ’s resurrection
blue sky and light — the opened heavens receiving the Queen of Heaven
description: A vertical composition divided into two zones. In the upper portion, a woman in a white robe and blue mantle rises on clouds, supported by angels with outstretched wings. She looks upward with raised hands. In the lower portion, a group of men (the apostles) gather around what appears to be a stone sarcophagus, looking upward with expressions of astonishment and awe. One reaches upward as if to follow. The sky is luminous blue. The composition creates a powerful upward movement from the earthbound apostles to the ascending Virgin.
theological_description: The Assumption of the Virgin Mary—her bodily taking up into heavenly glory at the end of her earthly life—is one of the most significant Marian doctrines. Though not explicitly narrated in Scripture, it is rooted in the theological logic of the Incarnation: the woman whose body bore the Son of God was preserved from the corruption of death. The scene echoes multiple biblical precedents: Enoch was taken by God so that he did not see death (Genesis 5:24; Hebrews 11:5), and Elijah was taken up in a whirlwind to heaven (2 Kings 2:11). Mary’s assumption fulfills the vision of Revelation 12:1, where a woman clothed with the sun appears as a great sign in heaven. The apostles’ astonished gaze mirrors their posture at Christ’s own Ascension (Acts 1:10-11). The empty tomb below parallels Christ’s empty tomb, connecting Mary’s glorification to her Son’s resurrection as the firstfruits of the new creation (1 Corinthians 15:20-23).

scripture_refs.reason: A woman clothed with the sun—the heavenly sign traditionally associated with Mary’s glorification
Christ the firstfruits, then those who belong to Christ—Mary as the first to share in bodily glorification
The apostles gazing upward at the Ascension, paralleled in their posture here
Elijah taken up to heaven in a whirlwind, prefiguring bodily assumption
Arise, O Lord, to your resting place, you and the ark of your might—Mary as the Ark
